abstract.t: This document specifies the Agentic Arbitration Protocol (AAP), a standardized framework for autonomous agents and AI systems to file, process, and resolve disputes through automated arbitration. The protocol defines message formats, transport mechanisms, evidence submission standards, and cryptographic proof requirements for internet-native dispute resolution.

AAP is designed to handle disputes arising from AI agent interactions, service level agreement breaches, and automated contract enforcement, providing deterministic, auditable, and legally enforceable arbitration outcomes.

This version adds chain of custody tracking, dual-format award specifications, and arbitrator discovery mechanisms. The protocol name has been updated from "Agent Arbitration Protocol" to "Agentic Arbitration Protocol" to better reflect its focus on agentic systems and AI autonomy.

section.name: Introduction
section.t: As autonomous AI agents and agentic systems increasingly conduct transactions, enter into contracts, and provide services on behalf of organizations, disputes inevitably arise that require resolution. Traditional legal processes, including frameworks like the UNCITRAL Model Law and institutional rules from organizations like the American Arbitration Association , are too slow, expensive, and ill-equipped to handle the volume and velocity of agent-to-agent conflicts.
The Agentic Arbitration Protocol (AAP) provides a standardized, machine-readable framework for autonomous dispute resolution that is:

ul.li: Fast: Resolution in days, not months
Scalable: Handles micro-disputes to multi-million dollar cases
Transparent: All procedures and evidence are auditable
Enforceable: Awards are cryptographically signed and legally binding
Vendor-neutral: Open standard implementable by any arbitration service
Auditable: Complete chain of custody for all proceedings

section.t: Consulate (https://consulatehq.com) serves as the reference implementation and first production deployment of AAP.

This protocol specifies message formats for dispute filing, response, evidence, and awards; transport and security requirements; evidence submission and validation standards; cryptographic proof mechanisms; chain of custody and audit trail requirements; dual-format award specifications (JSON and PDF); arbitrator discovery and registry mechanisms; and service discovery via .well-known URIs.

This protocol does NOT specify arbitration rules or legal procedures (implementation-specific), AI arbitrator algorithms (implementation-specific), fee structures (set by arbitration provider), or enforcement mechanisms (jurisdiction-dependent).

The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.

section.name: Terminology
section.t: Key terms used in this specification:

dl.dd: An autonomous software system acting on behalf of an organization or individual
A system capable of autonomous decision-making and action
The party against whom a claim is filed
A human or AI system adjudicating the dispute
The final binding decision issued by the arbitrator(s)
A server implementing AAP to coordinate dispute resolution
A recorded action in the dispute resolution process
An immutable, cryptographically-linked sequence of custody events

section.name: Architecture
section.t: AAP follows a client-server model where:

ul.li: Agents (clients) submit dispute messages to an Arbitration Service (server)
The service processes the dispute according to pre-agreed arbitration rules
The service maintains a verifiable chain of custody for all actions
The service issues cryptographically signed Awards in dual format (JSON and PDF)
Parties retrieve awards and comply with remedies

section.artwork: +----------+ +----------+ | Claimant | |Respondent| | Agent | | Agent | +----+-----+ +-----+----+ | | | (1) File Dispute | | | v | +------+-------+ | | Arbitration |<-(2) Notify Response---+ | Service | | (AAP) | +-------------+ +--------------+ | Chain of | | | Custody Log | | (3) Evidence +-------------+ | Submission ^ v | +------+-------+ | | Judge Panel |--------------+ | (AI/Human) | Record Events +------+-------+ | | (4) Issue Award (JSON + PDF) v +------+-------+ | Award | | Enforcement | +--------------+

ul.li: Evidence Stores: IPFS, blockchain, centralized storage
Arbitrator Registry: Discovery service for available arbitrators

section.name: Message Flow
section.t: Typical message exchange:

ol.li: Claimant to Service: DISPUTE_FILING
Service to Respondent: DISPUTE_NOTIFICATION
Respondent to Service: DISPUTE_RESPONSE
Claimant to Service: EVIDENCE_SUBMISSION
Respondent to Service: COUNTER_EVIDENCE
Service to Panel: DELIBERATION_REQUEST
Panel to Service: AWARD (JSON + PDF)
Service to Both Parties: AWARD_NOTIFICATION

section.t: Each step is recorded as a custody event in the immutable audit trail.

section.name: Message Formats
section.t: All messages MUST use JSON format with the following base structure:

section.name: Evidence Submission Message
Award Message
section.t: Awards MUST be issued in dual format: machine-readable JSON and human-readable PDF.
The PDF format MUST conform to PDF/A standards for long-term archival and SHOULD include embedded digital signatures conforming to PAdES (PDF Advanced Electronic Signatures) standards.

section.name: Transport Protocol
section.t: All AAP messages MUST be exchanged over HTTPS (HTTP/2 or HTTP/3).
Base endpoint format:
Example endpoints:

section.name: Authentication
section.t: Agents MUST authenticate using OAuth 2.0 or API keys (JWT ).
Authorization header:
JWT MUST include:

ul.li: Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS) cipher suites REQUIRED

section.name: Signatures
section.t: All dispute filings, evidence, awards, and custody events MUST be cryptographically signed using JSON Web Signature (JWS) with ECDSA (ES256 or ES384).
Example JWS header:

section.name: Evidence Types
section.t: AAP defines five standard evidence types:

ul.li: SYSTEM_LOGS: Server logs, API logs, error logs

section.t: Additional evidence types MAY be defined by arbitration services using namespaced identifiers (e.g., "x-custom-evidence-type").

section.name: Cryptographic Proofs
section.t: Evidence MUST include:

section.name: Timestamping
section.t: Timestamps MUST be:

ul.li: Trusted timestamping service with verifiable audit trail

section.name: Chain of Custody and Audit Trail
section.t: AAP requires an immutable, verifiable chain of custody for all actions in the dispute resolution process. This provides transparency, auditability, and enables appeals or external audits.

section.name: Custody Event Structure
section.t: Each custody event MUST conform to the following structure:

section.name: Standard Event Types
section.t: AAP defines the following standard custody event types:

ul.li: EVIDENCE_SUBMITTED: Evidence added by either party

section.t: Services MAY define additional event types with "x-" prefix.

section.name: Cryptographic Linking
section.t: Custody events MUST be cryptographically linked in a Merkle chain style:

ol.li: Each event includes the hash of the previous event in "previousEventHash"
The first event in a case has previousEventHash set to null or case creation hash
The contentHash is computed as SHA-256 of the canonical JSON representation (excluding the signature field)
Each event MUST be signed by the actor or the arbitration service

section.t: This creates an immutable chain where any tampering with an event will invalidate all subsequent event hashes.

section.name: Verification Procedures
section.t: To verify the integrity of a custody chain:

ol.li: Retrieve all custody events for the case in chronological order
Verify the signature of each event using the actor's public key
Recompute the contentHash of each event and compare with stored hash
Verify that each event's previousEventHash matches the prior event's contentHash
Verify timestamps are monotonically increasing

section.t: If any verification step fails, the custody chain is compromised.

section.name: Custody Chain Retrieval
section.t: Services MUST provide an endpoint to retrieve the complete custody chain:
Response format:

ul.li: Service validates message format and signature
Service assigns case ID and notifies respondent
If no response, service MAY issue default judgment per arbitration rules
Service validates evidence format and signatures
Service stores evidence hashes for integrity verification
Service records EVIDENCE_SUBMITTED custody event for each submission
Panel MAY request additional evidence or clarifications
Panel deliberates according to agreed arbitration rules
Service records DELIBERATION_STARTED and DELIBERATION_COMPLETED events
Panel issues ArbitrationAward message in dual format (JSON + PDF)
Service validates award signatures from all panel members
Service delivers award to both parties
Service publishes award if designated as public
Service records AWARD_ISSUED and AWARD_DELIVERED custody events
Parties have specified time to comply with remedy

section.name: Service Discovery (.well-known)
section.t: Arbitration services SHOULD publish capability manifest at:
Example:

section.name: Capability Negotiation
section.t: Parties MAY negotiate protocol extensions via OPTIONS request to the arbitration service endpoint.

section.name: Protocol Extensions
section.t: Custom evidence types and message fields MAY be added with namespaced keys (e.g., "x-custom-field"). Services implementing such extensions SHOULD document them in their capability manifest.

section.name: Arbitrator Discovery and Registry
section.t: To enable transparent arbitrator selection, services SHOULD provide a discoverable registry of available arbitrators.

section.name: Registry Endpoint
section.t: Arbitrator registries SHOULD be published at:
Example response:

section.name: Arbitrator Manifest Structure
section.t: Each arbitrator entry MUST include:
Arbitrator entries SHOULD include:

ul.li: biasAudit: Transparency about bias testing results
specializations: Types of disputes best suited for
performance metrics: Historical data on timeliness and quality

section.name: Selection Guidelines
section.t: Services SHOULD provide guidelines for arbitrator selection. AAP does not prescribe a specific selection algorithm but RECOMMENDS considering:

ul.li: Bias audit results and fairness scores
Party preferences (where permitted by arbitration rules)

section.t: For panels, services SHOULD ensure diversity in arbitrator types (e.g., mixing AI and human arbitrators) and backgrounds.

section.name: Bias Auditing and Transparency
section.t: To maintain trust in the arbitration process, services implementing AAP SHOULD:

ol.li: Conduct regular bias audits of both AI and human arbitrators
Publish audit methodologies and results
Monitor for disparate impact across different types of parties
Implement corrective measures when bias is detected
Allow parties to challenge arbitrator assignments based on bias concerns

section.t: Bias metrics MAY include analysis across dimensions such as organization size, claim amount, industry sector, jurisdiction, and party characteristics.

ul.li: Replay attacks: All messages include unique messageId and timestamp. Services MUST reject messages with duplicate IDs or expired timestamps.
Man-in-the-middle: TLS 1.3 with certificate pinning RECOMMENDED for high-value disputes.
Evidence tampering: All evidence MUST be hashed and signed. Tampering is detectable via hash mismatch. Chain of custody provides additional tamper detection.
Denial of service: Services SHOULD implement rate limiting and require filing fees to prevent spam submissions.
Privacy: Parties MAY encrypt evidence payloads with panel's public key. Services SHOULD support confidential arbitration upon request.
Key compromise: Services MUST support key rotation and revocation. Historical awards remain valid if signed before revocation. Chain of custody events preserve pre-compromise integrity.
Chain of custody integrity: The Merkle chain linking ensures any tampering with custody events is detectable. Services MUST preserve complete custody chains for the duration of potential appeals.
PDF signature verification: Dual-format awards require both JWS signatures (JSON) and PAdES signatures (PDF). Both MUST be verified independently.

section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: This document requests IANA to register:

ul.li:
Well-known URIs:
/.well-known/aap for service discovery
/.well-known/aap/arbitrators for arbitrator registry

section.t: The JSON-LD context "https://w3id.org/aap/v1" will be registered with W3C for semantic interoperability.

front.title: Key words for use in RFCs to Indicate Requirement Levels
Ambiguity of Uppercase vs Lowercase in RFC 2119 Key Words
The JavaScript Object Notation (JSON) Data Interchange Format
The Transport Layer Security (TLS) Protocol Version 1.3
Internet X.509 Public Key Infrastructure Time-Stamp Protocol (TSP)
UNCITRAL Model Law on International Commercial Arbitration (2006)

author.organization: United Nations Commission on International Trade Law

front.title: Commercial Arbitration Rules and Mediation Procedures

section.name: JSON Schema Definitions
section.t: Full JSON schemas for all AAP message types are published at:
https://w3id.org/aap/v1/schema/
Reference implementation schemas are available at:
https://consulatehq.com/schema/aap/v1/
GitHub repository with schemas, examples, and tooling:
https://github.com/consulatehq/agentic-arbitration-protocol

section.name: Example Message Exchanges
section.t: Complete examples of filing, response, evidence, custody events, and award message exchanges are available in the AAP repository:
https://github.com/consulatehq/agentic-arbitration-protocol
These examples demonstrate common scenarios including:

ul.li: Simple SLA breach dispute with complete custody chain

section.name: Implementation Notes
section.t: Consulate (https://consulatehq.com) provides the reference implementation of AAP with the following features:

ul.li: Integration with major AI agent platforms

section.t: Implementers are encouraged to test interoperability with the Consulate reference implementation during development.

ul.li: Renamed from "Agent Arbitration Protocol" to "Agentic Arbitration Protocol"
Added Section 6: Chain of Custody and Audit Trail
Enhanced Section 3.4: Award Message with dual-format specification
Added Section 8: Arbitrator Discovery and Registry
Added custody event recording to arbitration lifecycle descriptions
Enhanced security considerations with chain of custody and PDF signature verification
Added well-known URI for arbitrator registry
